event: The earliest Archaic-period (Ortoiroid) hunter-gatherers from South America are thought to have reached Dominica, leaving the island's first human traces.
Arawak-speaking Saladoid peoples migrate from the Orinoco region and settle Dominica, introducing pottery, agriculture, and village life.
Kalinago (Island Carib) peoples expand through the Lesser Antilles, gradually displacing the earlier Taino/Arawak inhabitants of Dominica.
Christopher Columbus sights the island on his second voyage on a Sunday (3 November), naming it Dominica after the Latin for the Lord's Day.
England's King Charles I grants Dominica, among other Caribbean islands, to the Earl of Carlisle, though no effective settlement follows.
England and France agree to leave Dominica and St. Vincent as neutral territories belonging to the Kalinago, in a treaty respecting Carib resistance.
French woodcutters from Martinique and Guadeloupe begin establishing timber camps and small settlements on Dominica.
France establishes formal control over Dominica, developing coffee plantations worked increasingly by enslaved Africans.
British forces capture Dominica from France during the Seven Years' War.
The Treaty of Paris formally cedes Dominica to Britain, which begins organizing it as a colony and parceling out plantation lands.
Roseau is established as the capital of British Dominica.
France recaptures Dominica during the American Revolutionary War.
The Treaty of Paris returns Dominica to British control at the end of the war.
Enslaved Africans launch a major rebellion against the plantation regime, reflecting persistent resistance on the island.
Maroon and rebel uprisings inspired by the French Revolution challenge British rule, leading to prolonged conflict in the interior.
A French expedition raids and burns much of Roseau, extracting a ransom before withdrawing in the final French attack on the island.
Britain's possession of Dominica is confirmed by the Treaty of Paris, ending French claims to the island.
The Brown Privilege Bill grants political and social rights to free people of color, opening the franchise to the free non-white population.
Slavery is abolished throughout the British Empire, beginning emancipation for Dominica's enslaved population under an apprenticeship system.
Full emancipation takes effect, and Dominica soon becomes the only British Caribbean colony with a black-majority elected legislative assembly in the 19th century.
Britain replaces the representative assembly with a part-elected, part-nominated council, curbing the influence of the black majority.
Dominica is incorporated into the federal colony of the Leeward Islands.
Dominica becomes a Crown Colony, with the British Crown taking direct administrative control over its government.
Britain formally recognizes Kalinago land by establishing the Carib Territory (Kalinago Territory) on the island's east coast.
The Carib (Kalinago) War, sparked by a customs dispute, ends with the colonial deposition of chief Jolly John and a temporary suspension of the chieftaincy.
Labor unrest spreading across the British West Indies prompts demands for political and social reform in Dominica.
Dominica is transferred from the Leeward Islands to the administration of the Windward Islands.
Universal adult suffrage is introduced, greatly expanding political participation in Dominica.
Dominica joins the West Indies Federation as a member territory.
Edward Oliver LeBlanc becomes Chief Minister, leading Dominica through the late colonial era and toward self-government.
The West Indies Federation collapses, and Dominica reverts to direct association with Britain.
Dominica becomes an associated state of the United Kingdom, gaining full internal self-government while Britain retains defense and external affairs.
Patrick John becomes Premier, leading the government in the final years before independence.
Dominica gains full independence from the United Kingdom on 3 November as the Commonwealth of Dominica, with Patrick John as its first Prime Minister.
Dominica is admitted to the United Nations on 18 December as its newest member state.
Hurricane David devastates the island, killing dozens and destroying much of the agricultural economy, while political unrest forces Patrick John from power.
Mary Eugenia Charles becomes Prime Minister, the first woman to hold the office in the Caribbean, leading the Dominica Freedom Party to victory.
Two coup attempts, including a mercenary plot tied to former PM Patrick John, are foiled, helping consolidate Charles's government.
Prime Minister Charles, as chair of the OECS, plays a leading role in inviting and supporting the United States-led intervention in Grenada.
Edison James and the United Workers' Party win elections, ending the long premiership of Eugenia Charles.
Roosevelt 'Rosie' Douglas of the Dominica Labour Party becomes Prime Minister but dies in office within months.
Roosevelt Skerrit becomes Prime Minister at age 31, beginning a long tenure leading the Dominica Labour Party.
An earthquake off the northern coast damages buildings in the Portsmouth area and surrounding communities.
Tropical Storm Erika causes catastrophic flooding and landslides, killing about 30 people and inflicting severe damage to infrastructure.
Hurricane Maria strikes as a Category 5 storm, devastating Dominica, causing dozens of deaths and destroying a vast share of homes and crops.
Dominica launches an ambitious recovery effort, pledging to become the world's first 'climate-resilient nation' after Hurricane Maria.
Prime Minister Roosevelt Skerrit's Dominica Labour Party wins a landslide in snap elections amid an opposition boycott over electoral reform.
